wb.hint: Draw on the left — it mirrors to the peer after a short network hop

ws.title: What's a WebSocket — and how does it connect?
ws.lead: A normal web request is one-and-done: the browser asks, the server answers, the line closes. A WebSocket keeps the line open both ways, so either side can send a message at any moment without asking again.
ws.step1: Your browser sends a normal HTTPS request asking to upgrade the connection to a WebSocket.
ws.step2: The server agrees, the protocol switches, and the connection stays open instead of closing.
ws.step3: Now either side can push messages instantly — so a pen stroke lands on every screen in milliseconds.

sys.title: System check
sys.desc: Curious how this page performs? Pop the hood — live runtime metrics, measured in your browser right now.
sys.foot: measured client-side · nothing leaves your browser

render.d: Components stream in and hydrate without blocking the main thread.

memory.d: Lazy chunks and clean unmounts keep memory flat between routes.

network.d: Three.js and the chat widget load in their own chunks, on demand.

paint.d: Fixed dimensions and a capped pixel ratio avoid reflow and CLS.

frame.d: Animations run on requestAnimationFrame and pause under reduced-motion.

a11y.d: Focus states, reduced-motion and full RTL are first-class here.

Designed, built and shipped solo on AWS serverless; already live with a paying client, with more onboarding.

bhanzu.intro: Bhanzu teaches math live to students at scale. My work sits where real-time media, backend services and AI meet: keep thousands of concurrent classrooms streaming smoothly, make the room genuinely interactive, and weave LLMs into the learning loop — all on infrastructure we own rather than rent.

streaming.title: A proprietary live-streaming platform that replaced Zoom
streaming.body:
I architected the live classroom on **Amazon IVS**, moving off per-seat Zoom licensing onto usage-based streaming we control end to end. The trainer's camera and mic are captured in-browser, transcoded by IVS into an adaptive-bitrate ladder, and delivered through the edge at sub-second latency.

The hard parts live at the edges: token-based joins, reconnection with backoff, and **adaptive bitrate** so weak connections degrade gracefully. The payoff was **~50% lower streaming spend**.
streaming.note: The teacher's video is captured in the browser, compressed into several quality levels, and sent through Amazon's network — so each student automatically gets the smoothest version their connection can handle. Like Netflix, but live.

whiteboard.title: A collaborative whiteboard that stays in sync, stroke by stroke
whiteboard.body:
I built a multi-user whiteboard on the **Canvas API** where every stroke becomes an **event broadcast over WebSockets**. A central hub fans each event to all peers, which re-apply it — so everyone sees the same board instantly.

It's modeled as an **event stream**, not a shared image: events are logged, so a late joiner replays history to rebuild the exact board. The interactivity drove **~30% higher engagement**.
whiteboard.note: When the teacher draws, that single action is sent as a tiny message to a central hub, which instantly copies it onto every student's board. Every action is also saved — so anyone who joins late watches the full board rebuild itself.

backend.title: Layered backend services on Python, Flask & DynamoDB
backend.body:
I own backend services on a **strict layered architecture** — route, then service, then repository — so logic stays testable and the data layer can be swapped without touching the rules above it.

Data lives in **DynamoDB with single-table, access-pattern-first design**, keeping reads single-digit-millisecond at scale. On top run REST APIs for student workflows, assignments, and a gamification engine.
backend.note: A request flows through clean, separated layers — one checks who you are, one runs the rules, one talks to the database. Because they're separate, any layer can be changed or tested without breaking the others.

rag.title: LLM features grounded in the real curriculum with RAG
rag.body:
I wired LLMs into the learning loop — **summarizing lessons**, **generating adaptive quizzes**, and giving **targeted feedback** — with careful prompt engineering.

What makes it trustworthy is **Retrieval-Augmented Generation**. Course content is indexed into a vector store; at question time the relevant passages are retrieved and composed into the prompt, so the model answers **from real material**, not from memory.
rag.note: Instead of trusting the AI to remember the syllabus, we first find the exact lesson pages that match the question, hand only those to the model, and ask it to answer from them — so replies stay accurate and on-topic.

microfrontend.title: A microfrontend platform teams can ship independently
microfrontend.body:
I structured the frontend as **microfrontends**: a host shell that owns routing, layout and auth, loading independently-built remotes at runtime via **module federation**. Classroom, assignments and gamification each live in their own repo with their own CI/CD.

They still feel like one product because they share a single **design system** and common packages. A team can deploy without waiting on — or breaking — the others.
microfrontend.note: The app is built from separate mini-apps — classroom, assignments, rewards — that each team ships on its own schedule. A shared design kit keeps them all looking and feeling like one product.

quality.title: Dashboards plus a test gate that halved production issues
quality.body:
I built the trainer and student dashboards — quizzes, assignments and real-time feedback in one place — then put a safety net under them.

I added **Vitest** unit tests and **Playwright** end-to-end tests to CI, so a change can't ship unless it passes the full suite and the type check. That cut **production issues ~50%**.
quality.note: Every code change has to pass an automatic checklist — unit tests, full click-through tests, and type checks — before it's allowed to go live. If anything fails, it never reaches students.

avataar.intro: Avataar turns products into interactive 3D. I worked on the artist-facing tooling that creates and renders those assets — making a heavy, GPU-bound workflow feel fast and predictable to the people using it every day.

tooling.title: 3D animation & rendering tooling that halved asset time
tooling.body:
I built artist-facing 3D tooling on **Three.js**, driven declaratively through **React Three Fiber**. Scene state lives in React; the reconciler diffs it into the Three.js scene graph — so updates are predictable instead of imperative WebGL calls.

By streamlining the create → preview → export loop, I cut the time to produce a render-ready asset by **~50%**.
tooling.note: Artists describe a 3D scene in simple React code, and the tool translates that step by step into instructions the graphics card can draw — turning a slow, manual process into an automatic one.

perf.title: Render pipelines and frontend, 40–50% faster
perf.body:
I profiled the render loop and attacked the real costs: **geometry instancing** to batch draw calls, memoized scene graphs, fewer re-renders, and lazy asset loading so the viewport stays responsive while heavy meshes stream in.

Together those changes improved performance and scalability by **40–50%**.
perf.note: By reusing shapes, avoiding repeated work, and loading heavy assets only when needed, each frame became far cheaper to draw — so the tool stays smooth even on big scenes.

components.title: A reusable component architecture for velocity
components.body:
I designed a **reusable component architecture** so common 3D and UI primitives — controls, materials, loaders — are composed rather than copy-pasted.

Features assemble from trusted building blocks, raising developer velocity and keeping the codebase maintainable as it grew.
components.note: Small reusable building blocks — controls, materials, loaders — snap together into bigger features, so new tools get assembled instead of rebuilt from scratch.

stayflexi.intro: Stayflexi runs the software hotels live in — property management, distribution to booking channels, and point of sale. Over three years I worked across that whole surface: a new revenue product, a new vertical, a scaling push, and a platform migration off legacy tech.

bidflexi.title: Bidflexi — a dynamic booking engine that lifted conversion
bidflexi.body:
I led **Bidflexi**, where guests bid on rooms against a dynamic pricing engine instead of a fixed rate. A bid runs through pricing rules and live inventory, which accepts it or returns a counter — then confirms the booking.

It opened a new **B2C growth channel** and gave price-sensitive guests a reason to convert.
bidflexi.note: A guest offers a price, the system checks it against live pricing rules and available rooms, then either accepts it or sends back a counter-offer — turning browsers into bookings.

pos.title: A React Native POS that opened the restaurant business
pos.body:
I architected a cross-platform **point-of-sale app in React Native**, extending Stayflexi from rooms into food & beverage on a single shared codebase. Logic is written once in TypeScript; the bridge renders real native iOS and Android components.

Hotels could run restaurants on the same platform as their rooms — a new vertical without two native teams.
pos.note: One app, written once, runs as a real native app on both iPhone and Android — so hotels could start taking restaurant orders without building two separate apps.

scale.title: PMS & OTA platforms scaled to 1,000+ hotels
scale.body:
I scaled the property-management and OTA-distribution platforms to **1,000+ hotels globally**. At that size, distribution is the hard problem: rates and inventory must stay consistent across many booking channels in near real time.

The platform is the hub — hotels push inventory in, and a channel manager fans rates out to every OTA, syncing bookings back.
scale.note: The platform is the central hub — every hotel's rooms and rates flow in, and it keeps them in sync across all the big booking sites in near real time, for 1,000+ hotels at once.

migration.title: Migrated off legacy Jython, and made the frontend 75% faster
migration.body:
I migrated the platform from **Jython to Django**, trading a dead-end runtime for a maintainable, modular architecture.

In parallel I refactored the frontend — **code splitting** the single bundle into route-level chunks, plus optimized state — for roughly **75% better performance**.
migration.note: I moved the system off an old, unsupported engine onto a modern one, and split the website into smaller pieces that load on demand — so it stays maintainable and loads much faster.
